Because if you spend too much time only with the disappointed, disappointment starts to feel like proof of intelligence. As if hope itself were a form of stupidity. I did not want to become that kind of man—the one who mistakes emotional ruin for depth.

And honestly, I made myself do small useful things when my mind wanted only analysis. Help review something carefully. Follow up on one practical issue. Speak to one person without unloading my whole philosophy of decline onto him. These acts do not solve the larger corruption, no. But they keep the soul from becoming addicted to diagnosis without service.

Spiritually, I tried to increase istighfar. Not as a ritual formula only, but as a way of breaking the secret alliance between my ego and my criticism. Because sometimes a man speaks truth while internally saying, “At least I am not like these people.” Astaghfirullah. Seeking forgiveness cuts this a little, or at least exposes it.

And I became more careful with what I consumed in conversation. There are forms of talk that make disappointment feel noble, almost luxurious. Endless cynical dissection, sarcastic certainty, that pleasure of all sitting together proving how finished everything is. I know this mood well. In Mosul, in offices, in homes, everywhere. Bas I started limiting how much I fed that side of myself. Not denying

[YOUR NEXT QUESTION]

conversation.text:
I replaced them with things that put me back in contact with obligation, not mood. هذا كان العلاج الأقرب. Bitterness feeds on repetition, on circles of talk where nothing is asked from you except a sharper sentence, a darker interpretation. So I needed forms of life that demanded something steadier.

First, quieter company. Not cheerful in a fake way—Allah yijibna min هالنوع—but people with sobriety, people who could acknowledge the rot without making it their main performance. A tea with someone who speaks of his work, his family, one practical difficulty, one practical step. This helped me. It reminded me that dignity is often very untheatrical. Not every honest man is loud, and not every wounded man is deep.

I also returned more deliberately to technical work, even in small ways. Reviewing plans carefully, checking quantities, looking at a structural problem as a structural problem. This may sound dry, bas it nourished me because it reintroduced proportion. Engineering, at its best, does not reward emotional exaggeration. Either the load path is sound or it is not. Either the detail will fail under stress or it will not. There is mercy in this. It cleans the mind from moral melodrama for a while and puts you back before reality.

And Qur’an, honestly. Not always with some grand spiritual state—sometimes just to sit and be corrected in scale. Because the world of disappointment makes your own analysis feel central, while revelation keeps reminding you that man is smaller, more mixed, more needy than he likes to believe. It does not erase injustice, of course not. But it prevents your outrage from becoming your lord.

I also found nourishment in small usefulness. This matters more than people think. To help someone with paperwork, to explain something to a younger engineer, to visit a relative properly, to follow through on a promise—these are not dramatic acts, but they return a man to moral gravity. They make him less dependent on the intoxication of commentary. Sometimes I think one reason bitterness grows is that it offers the feeling of significance without the burden of service.

And there was another thing: beauty without argument. Walking at a quiet hour. Sitting on a rooftop in the evening. Hearing ordinary life continue—children, vendors, the call to prayer. In Mosul, after everything, even the persistence of ordinary life can be corrective. It says: not everything is reducible to your judgment. There is still creation, still mercy, still human continuity. وهذه نعمة

INTERVIEWER: Tell me about yourself and what brought you here today.
INTERVIEWER: When you say you’re afraid of “injustice done in the name of justice,” where do you think that fear comes from most deeply—something you witnessed in others, or something you recognized first in yourself?
SUBJECT: Mostly in myself, بصراحة، and that is precisely why it frightens me. It is easy—too easy—to notice this disease in others. A man sees someone become rigid, severe, enjoying the performance of righteousness, and he says: yes, this is ugly. But when the same tendency begins in your own chest, it arrives with arguments, with noble language, with very clean explanations. That is what makes it dangerous.

I have seen it in others too, of course. In religious speech, in politics, in professional life—people using the vocabulary of principle while humiliating others, or reducing them, or acting as if correctness itself gives them permission to stop being just. This exists. كثيرا. But what struck me more deeply was recognizing the seed of it in myself when I would speak the truth about something real—corruption, cowardice, compromise—and feel, mixed into that truth, a kind of satisfaction. Not satisfaction that truth was served, but satisfaction that I had remained untainted. وهذه نية فاسدة if a man leaves it unchecked.

That recognition disturbed me. Because then the issue is no longer simply “Am I right or wrong?” A person can be right in content and wrong in soul. He can identify the rot accurately while being inwardly nourished by contempt. And once that happens, justice becomes a mirror for the ego. You are no longer defending what is right; you are feeding on distinction.

Maybe this comes also from my environment, from what I have lived around in Mosul, in institutions that speak one language and practice another. When you see hypocrisy long enough, you become determined not to be false. This determination is good in origin. Bas sometimes it hardens into an identity of “I am not like them,” and that identity can become spiritually intoxicating. Then you start needing other people’s compromise in order to keep feeling morally clear yourself. وهذا خطير جدا.

So yes, I witnessed examples outside. But the deeper fear came when I realized I was not immune. That under pressure, loneliness, disappointment, even legitimate moral pain, I too could turn truth into a weapon that exceeds its proper measure. I could enjoy being the one who sees clearly. I could become less interested in reform than in indictment.

And once a man sees that in himself, if he is honest, he cannot be casual anymore. He has to watch his tone, his intentions, even the secret pleasures he takes in being opposed. Otherwise he will commit ظلم while speaking against ظلم. And
INTERVIEWER: And when you noticed that “secret pleasure” in being the one who sees clearly, what helped you resist building your identity around it?
SUBJECT: What helped me most was humiliation, in the healthy sense—not degradation, but being brought back to size. هذا أهم شيء. Life has a way of exposing to you that seeing clearly is not the same as being pure. You may diagnose a sickness correctly and still carry another sickness in your intention. When I began to notice that secret pleasure, I also began to notice how unproductive it was. It did not make me braver in any useful way. It made me sharper, yes, but also narrower. More easily disgusted, less able to build anything with anyone. And as someone from engineering, I cannot ignore this language: if a stance cannot bear load, if it only stands beautifully in theory, then something is wrong in the structure.

Also, I remembered that identity built on opposition is parasitic. It needs failure around it to keep feeding itself. This is miserable. If I become “the man who sees clearly” as my main self-understanding, then I quietly start needing others to remain blind, corrupt, compromised—because their condition confirms my role. Astaghfirullah, what kind of moral life is that? It means my ego has entered even my disgust with ego. A trap.

What resisted that in me, partly, was trying to return from posture to duty. Not “Who am I in this story?” but “What is required from me here?” Sometimes what is required is to speak plainly.
